title: Best Brass Cabinet Pulls of 2026: Finishes That Match & Screws That Fit
excerpt: Find the best brass cabinet pulls that actually fit your cabinets, with finishes that match your fixtures—without surprise hardware store trips.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Find the best brass cabinet pulls that actually fit your cabinets, with finishes that match your fixtures—without surprise hardware store trips.
intro:
Brass cabinet pulls are the easiest way to elevate a kitchen, but the shopping experience is full of surprises—screws that are too short, ‘champagne bronze’ that looks pink, and hollow pulls that feel nothing like the solid weight you expected.

This guide cuts through that confusion by focusing on the details that matter after you open the box: which finishes actually coordinate with popular fixture brands, which pulls deliver that satisfying heft, and which screw lengths you’ll really need for your drawers.

Whether you’re refreshing a rental kitchen on a budget or adding ornate hardware to a French country dresser, understanding these differences keeps you from ordering—and returning—twice.

products.title: Franklin Brass (10-Pack) – Champagne Bronze Cabinet Handles, 3 Inch Hole Center, Modern Pulls for Drawers, Kitchen Cabinet Door, Dressers | Solid Bar
products.label: Franklin Brass Solid Bar
products.oneLiner: Solid alloy steel bar pull with champagne bronze finish that matches Delta faucets.
pros: Heavy, solid alloy steel construction with refined machining and detailing — 237 mentions, 97% positive
High-end appearance that easily passes for pricier hardware — 208 mentions, 95% positive
Finish resists scratches and scuffs even with daily kitchen use — 117 mentions, 95% positive
cons: Champagne bronze can appear more silvery or pinkish under certain lighting, not a true warm brass tone — 109 mentions on color, 24% negative — champagne bronze varies under lighting
products.verdict: A substantial, well-made pull that brings heavy-duty feel and lasting finish to a budget-friendly price, provided the champagne bronze's subtle silvery undertone under certain lights works for your space.
products.review:
The Franklin Brass Solid Bar pull delivers heavy, solid alloy steel construction that feels substantial in hand — a quality rarely found at this per-piece price point. The champagne bronze finish resists scratches and scuffs through daily use, maintaining a high-end look that mimics far costlier hardware. It also matches Delta champagne bronze faucets closely, simplifying coordinated kitchen or bath updates. Once installed, the pulls stay rigid with no flex or bending, and they come with screws that fit most standard drawer fronts right out of the box.

This 10-pack is best for homeowners refreshing kitchens or bathrooms with champagne bronze fixtures who want solid, weighty hardware without splurging. Under some lighting, the champagne bronze can appear more silvery or pinkish than a true warm brass, which may clash if you're aiming for a pure bright brass or gold tone. This color shift is most noticeable under cool LED task lighting, while warmer ambient light brings out the bronze. If you're matching other champagne bronze pieces from the same brand or Delta, the finish will blend well, but it's worth verifying against your existing fixtures in your own light.

The adjustable slide mechanism expands from 1-3/8 inches to 4 inches, letting you fit non-standard hole spacing without drilling new holes. Unlike solid bar pulls that demand exact matching, this telescoping design adapts to old furniture, rental cabinets, or any setup where patching and redrilling aren't an option. The champagne bronze finish gives a polished look, but the zinc construction prioritizes flexibility over heavy heft. Screws can strip if forced, so gentle installation is essential, but the tradeoff is access to a pull that works where others won't.

💡 💡 Tip: Test screws on a spare hole first; if resistance builds, back off and gently seat them to avoid stripping.

buyingGuide.heading: Material: Solid vs. Hollow
buyingGuide.body:
Most pulls labeled ‘brass’ are zinc alloy or stainless steel with a brass-plated finish, not solid brass. Solid zinc alloy pulls feel heavy and dense, resisting dents and lasting decades; hollow steel pulls are lighter and more budget-friendly but can ring or flex under hard use.

If you open a drawer every day, solid construction rewards you with a reassuring, premium feel—important for main kitchen cabinets. For low-traffic areas like a guest bathroom vanity, hollow pulls look identical and save significantly.

buyingGuide.heading: Finish: Brushed Brass, Champagne Bronze, Antique
buyingGuide.body:
The same finish name can look wildly different between brands. Brushed brass has a warm gold tone with a matte sheen, while champagne bronze often appears more muted—sometimes leaning silvery or pinkish depending on lighting. Antique brass introduces a darkened, aged patina that works beautifully with wood and oil-rubbed bronze fixtures.

Matching your pulls to existing faucets or light fixtures is the trickiest part. Delta champagne bronze, for example, coordinates reliably with Franklin Brass champagne bronze pulls but may clash with rose-gold-toned alternatives. Order a single pack first to test the color against your cabinetry and lighting before committing to a full set.

buyingGuide.heading: Center-to-Center Spacing
buyingGuide.body:
This is the distance between the two screw holes. Standard spacing is 3 inches or 5 inches; using the wrong measurement forces you to drill new holes or leaves mismatched gaps. Measure from the center of one hole to the center of the other—never edge-to-edge.

If your cabinets have non-standard spacing leftover from an earlier era, adjustable pulls save the day by expanding or contracting to fit existing holes without patching and redrilling.

buyingGuide.heading: Included Screw Length
buyingGuide.body:
The screws that ship with pulls are often only long enough for thin doors—a common frustration. Thicker drawer fronts or face-frame cabinets require longer screws to pass through the panel and pull base.

Before you order, measure total thickness: door front plus any overlay, then add about half an inch. If the included screws fall short, 1-inch or 1.5-inch machine screws from the hardware store fix the problem for pennies.

buyingGuide.heading: Style: Bar, Cup, Arch & More
buyingGuide.body:
Bar pulls offer a modern, clean line and comfortable grip for daily use. Cup pulls deliver traditional charm but can pinch fingers on deep drawers if the lip is shallow. Arch and bow pulls add decorative flourish for vintage or French country looks, while T-bar and square profiles lean contemporary.

Consider finger clearance—especially for cup and arch styles on drawers you open constantly. A pull that looks stunning but scrapes knuckles will annoy you every day.

faq.question: Why are the screws that come with cabinet pulls often too short?
faq.answer: Most pulls ship with screws sized for thin cabinet doors, but drawers and thicker fronts need longer lengths across the panel and face. Measure your door/drawer thickness plus pull depth, then pickup 1-inch mounting screws if the included ones don't reach; they cost pennies at any hardware store.

faq.question: What's the difference between brushed brass and champagne bronze?
faq.answer: Brushed brass has a warm gold tone with a matte finish, while champagne bronze is slightly more muted, sometimes with a silvery or pink undertone. Champagne bronze often coordinates with Delta fixtures, but lighting shifts its appearance—always test a sample first to ensure it matches your room.

faq.question: Do hollow cabinet pulls feel cheap?
faq.answer: Hollow stainless steel pulls are lighter and may feel less substantial than solid zinc or alloy, but they still look great and function fine. If you want the reassuring weight, choose pulls described as solid or heavy duty, and avoid those marketed as lightweight or hollow.

faq.question: How do I measure center-to-center spacing for cabinet pulls?
faq.answer: Measure the distance between the centers of the two mounting holes—not edge-to-edge. Standard spacing is 3 inches or 5 inches; if your existing holes don't match standard sizes, consider adjustable pulls that expand to fit without new drilling.
